danger_signals.visual_form: Uniformed Soviet soldiers with distinct sprite animations, varied by weapon type (gunners, flamethrowers, jetpack soldiers)
danger_signals.meaning: Active combat threat; different enemy types signal different attack patterns and danger levels

danger_signals.visual_form: Soldiers descending with parachutes from top of screen
danger_signals.meaning: Airborne threat; indicates stage-specific hazard patterns
Close-range melee threat; aggressive pursuit behavior

danger_signals.visual_form: Flying enemy sprites distinct from ground soldiers
danger_signals.meaning: Three-dimensional threat space; requires evasion in vertical plane

danger_signals.visual_form: Static environmental hazards in specific stage locations
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental danger; destruction reveals secret passages (FDS version)

danger_signals.visual_form: Specialized enemy variant with animated fire projectiles
Implicit mechanic; no visual countdown shown to player
danger_signals.meaning: Pressure signal; world-state urgency without explicit visual representation

safety_signals.visual_form: Dropped by defeated enemy soldiers; distinct pickup sprites
safety_signals.meaning: Tactical advantage; temporary offensive capability increase

safety_signals.visual_form: Temporary limited-duration visual effect on player character
safety_signals.meaning: Brief vulnerability immunity; world-state protection window

safety_signals.visual_form: Power-up item sprite; infinite ammunition supply visual
safety_signals.meaning: Resource security; removes supply scarcity temporarily

safety_signals.visual_form: Score milestone achievement; UI life counter increments
safety_signals.meaning: Safety margin expansion; resource accumulation for continued survival

safety_signals.visual_form: Implicit level progression; respawn position advancement
safety_signals.meaning: Progress preservation; reduces mission restart penalty

safety_signals.visual_form: Dual character sprites on screen simultaneously
safety_signals.meaning: Safety through teamwork; shared threat burden

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Player character(s) named Steve and Ben; military uniform visual
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Western military base facilities, prison structures (in rescue mission context)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Multiple distinct enemy soldier types with uniform visual language
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Basic soldiers, gunners, supply runners, combat specialists, jetpack soldiers, parachute gunners, gyrocopters, flame throwers, German Shepherd handlers
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Military base stages: missile base, warehouse, harbor, airport, headquarters; industrial/military architecture

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Prison structures visible in stage design; captive soldiers implied through narrative framing
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: NPC soldiers execute firing squad behavior at stage end if player fails to intervene

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Mission objective - Destroy secret weapon (NES Western version)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Nuclear missile visual in final stage headquarters; distinct weapons facility architecture
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Final boss encounter shifted to rocket launcher disarmament sequence

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enemy density increases; more dangerous enemy types appear in later stages
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Soviet soldiers spawn more frequently; 'more dangerous enemy types more numerous in western version'

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Soviet vs. American military iconography; base infiltration environment
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Continuous enemy assault; defensive perimeter behavior by Soviet forces

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Supply runner enemy type appears; carries ammunition/resources
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Defeat of supply runners yields weapon power-ups; reduces enemy resource availability

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Rescued POWs salute player; stage clear screen with stats display
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game loops to next difficulty; enemy patterns reset with increased challenge

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Life counter decreases in HUD; 'Player Out' jingle plays (NES version higher pitch than FDS)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Respawn at checkpoint (Western) or death location (Japanese); enemy waves continue

environmental_storytelling.example: Missile Base stage conveys Soviet military industrial capacity; Harbor stage suggests coastal infiltration route; Airport stage indicates supply logistics; Headquarters final stage escalates to strategic command center

environmental_storytelling.technique: Enemy type distribution as geopolitical indicator
environmental_storytelling.example: Jetpack soldiers appear in stages 4-5, suggesting advanced technology deployment in later mission phases; Parachute gunners indicate air superiority concerns; Gyrocopters represent Soviet air assets
Secret passages revealed by landmine destruction in stages 2, 4, 5 convey concealed base infrastructure; tunnel encounters with standard enemies plus supply runners suggest supply line networks
Flamethrowers, RPGs, grenades captured from Soviet soldiers; indicates enemy loadout distribution and player tactical adaptation
Star invincibility mark and unlimited pistol appear as temporary narrative breathing room; distinguish from permanent weapon upgrades
Lives awarded at 30,000 and 70,000 points; reinforces survival/attrition narrative without explicit story beats

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Semi-random spawn from screen edges; continuous deployment throughout stages
Pursuit and melee/projectile attack; spawning rate 'much more frequent in western version'
Sustained ranged fire pattern; specific to later stages
Aerial approach; stage 4-5 exclusive with varied sprite design between planned (stages 1-3) and actual appearance
Carries resource pickup on defeat; less frequent in western version
Three-dimensional mobility; requires vertical space evasion
Aggressive pursuit; close-range melee threat; handler-paired behavior
Salute animation at stage end (Japanese/arcade); signals mission success
Creates fire zones; alternate final stage threat replacing paratrooper unit in NES version

notes: Rush'n Attack uses implicit world-state communication through enemy type density, stage architecture, and mission context shifts rather than explicit UI/HUD signals. The Cold War framing (US Green Berets vs Soviet forces) establishes faction identity through character role designation and enemy military uniform language. Regional differences (Japanese vs NES Western) show intentional difficulty/pacing adjustments: western version increases enemy spawning frequency, grants more starting lives (5 vs 3), and enhances weapon pickup value (3 uses vs 1). The invisible time limit creates pressure without visual manifestation—a unique tension mechanism. Power-ups function as safety signals through both offensive (weapons) and defensive (invincibility) capabilities. The FDS version's secret tunnel system accessed via landmine destruction adds hidden world-state layers not present in cartridge version. Stage progression visually communicates escalating threat through enemy composition shifts and architectural complexity increase.
